channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.gamedeveloper.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.gamedeveloper.com

item.title: Blue Prince's somber tone was inspired by Myst, says creator
item.content:encoded:
Image via Dogubomb/Raw Fury

Spoiler alert: this story contains a small narrative spoiler for Blue Prince

The intricate puzzle design of Dogubomb's Blue Prince made it one of the most celebrated games of 2025. And the accolades for its excellence are still pouring in, with creator Tonda Ros accepting the awards for "Outstanding Achievement in Game Design" and "Outstanding Achievement for an Independent Game" at the 2026 DICE Awards. But it was not only the randomized mansion layout and multilayered mysteries that made the game stick in player's minds. The mysteries at the heart of the mansion played an important role. As the player learns to optimize their run and plunge deeper and deeper into Mt. Holly Estate, they piece together the history of the Sinclair family and learn how it crumbled under backstabbing, betrayal, and intrigue.

This is not told through flashbacks and exposition. It's a tale pieced together through notes, photographs, letters, and a quiet-yet-unsettling score from composers Trigg & Gusset. Even though the player character is ostensibly on this journey to claim their inheritance and ownership of the mansion, little feels celebratory about what they uncover. A mood like this takes careful work to craft—too somber, and a player may put the game down, tired of being frustrated by puzzles and a morose game.

Related:Book excerpt: The Game Narrative Kaleidoscope

But if it's not somber enough, the sense of mystery might fade, and the game may lose its grip on more casual puzzle fans. Where then, did Ros take inspiration for setting such a specific mood?

Speaking to Game Developer after he accepted both of his awards (and constructed an elaborate photography display for the benefit of Game File's Stephen Totilo), Ros explained that the inspiration for Blue Prince's tone was none other than the classic adventure game Myst, by Cyan Games. "The atmosphere was poetically calm, but there's a rich history there," he explained. "It's the environmental storytelling of a past that is just out of the player's reach—and yet there is such meaning behind this world."

"In Blue Prince's case...the impetus of the story is tragic. Your great uncle has died. But there's something beyond that fact that I think players can just intuitively feel."

Capturing the grief of a life fully-lived

Blue Prince doesn't dump this moodiness on the drop of a hat. It's likely they'll spend a few hours noodling over pleasant puzzles and cursing the random room generation system before unlocking an area called "The Tomb." The Tomb is a gateway into many of the manor's secrets, and for me, it was the moment where the game crossed over from being a fun puzzle mystery and into something more heartbreaking.

Related:Hades devs: accidentally inventing a subgenre was 'surreal'

In the tomb, players find a letter from Herbert Sinclair addressed directly to the character they're inhabiting. Its tone differs from previous missives they've found. "Simon, so here's where it all ends. At least for me," it begins. "And of all the picturesque places of this grand estate, I chose this dreary dank dungeon to be laid to rest... And that same dim somber fate fell upon my father and his father before him who loved the outdoors even more than myself. They say there is no stronger bond of servitude than tradition. And family tradition? Thrice as strong. These are chains that seldom bend and never break."

One line in particular sent a wave of sorrow over me: "In our youth, we are gifted with dreams and ambitions. At our death, we are left with realities and regrets."

Sinclair goes on to rage against the idea of carrying on tradition for tradition's sake, lamenting that he had to be buried in a sunless tomb and not under a beautiful oak tree. He exhorts the player character to break away from mindlessly honoring the Sinclair family's methods of living. But then strangely, he urges the player to build a shrine and turn the tomb into "hollow ground"—and bury himself within, leaving a letter to the next heir, just as he did.

Related:Battlefield 6's meta progression needed 'a little bit of a rework,' says producer

It feels as if in a few short paragraphs, Sinclair relived his own life's arc: to dream of subverting tradition, only to encourage it at the end of one's days. Quite a tonal shift for players who grew accustomed to the thrill of drawing and rerolling rooms in the manor above.

About the Author

Senior Editor, GameDeveloper.com

Bryant Francis is a writer, journalist, and narrative designer based in Boston, MA. He currently writes for Game Developer, a leading B2B publication for the video game industry. His credits include Proxy Studios' 4X strategy game Zephon, Iron Anchor Studios' Down With The Ship, and Amplitude Studio's 2017 game Endless Space 2.



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/design/blue-prince-s-somber-tone-was-inspired-by-myst
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
